title: Gas exchange
summary: Gas exchange is where the physics of diffusion meets the anatomy of living things. This lesson builds the whole B3.1 picture: the properties every effective gas-exchange surface must share, why surface-area-to-volume ratio forces larger organisms to evolve specialised surfaces, and how those properties appear concretely in the human alveolus, the fish gill and the leaf. Ventilation and blood flow are treated as the machinery that keeps the concentration gradient steep, and every worked example and explain question is laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards Paper 2 marks — point by point, adaptation linked to effect, with error-carried-forward — so you can see how the way you write your answer protects your score.
learningObjectives: State the properties of an effective gas-exchange surface and explain why each one speeds diffusion.
Relate surface-area-to-volume ratio to the need for specialised exchange surfaces and ventilation in larger organisms.
Describe the structure and adaptations of the alveolus and the mechanism of ventilation in the human lungs.
Compare gas exchange in human lungs with the counter-current system of fish gills and the leaf of a plant.
Present an explanation so that each distinct adaptation is linked to its effect on diffusion, the way marks are actually awarded.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Why living things need a good swapping surface
simpleExplanation.summary: Every cell needs oxygen delivered and carbon dioxide taken away, and both gases move only by diffusion. Diffusion is fast over tiny distances but hopeless over large ones, so organisms bigger than a few cells have to build a special surface — lungs, gills or a leaf's air spaces — that is large, thin, moist and permeable, and keep fresh supply flowing past it.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of gas exchange like a busy loading dock. Diffusion is a porter who can only walk a very short distance, so the dock has to be enormous (huge surface area) and the wall between truck and warehouse paper-thin (short diffusion distance). If deliveries piled up on the dock the porters would stop bothering to move goods, so trucks keep pulling in and pulling out (ventilation) and forklifts keep clearing the warehouse side (blood flow) — that constant traffic keeps a steep difference between the two sides so the porters never rest.
steps: Gases cross exchange surfaces only by diffusion, and diffusion is quick only across thin, large, moist surfaces.
As an organism grows, its volume rises faster than its surface area, so its outer surface can no longer supply its inner cells.
Larger organisms therefore evolve a specialised exchange surface with a large area and a short diffusion path.
Ventilation (moving the medium) and blood flow (moving the internal fluid) keep replacing the gases on each side, holding the concentration gradient steep so diffusion keeps going.

flashcards.back: Large surface area; thin (short diffusion distance); moist; permeable to gases; and a maintained concentration gradient (via ventilation and blood flow). Each property maps onto a factor in Fick's law of diffusion.
Rate of diffusion is proportional to (surface area × concentration difference) ÷ diffusion distance. So a good exchange surface maximises area and gradient and minimises thickness.

flashcards.front: Why the surface must be thin
flashcards.back: A short diffusion distance (one cell thick) means gases have less far to travel, so they diffuse across faster. The alveolar wall plus capillary wall is typically only ~0.5 µm.

flashcards.front: Why the surface must be moist
flashcards.back: Oxygen and carbon dioxide must dissolve in a thin film of liquid before they can diffuse across the cell membrane. A dry surface would halt gas exchange.

Ventilation continually replaces the air (or water) at the surface, keeping a high oxygen and low carbon dioxide concentration on the outside. This MAINTAINS the concentration gradient so diffusion does not slow and stop.

As an object gets larger its volume rises faster than its surface area, so SA:V falls. A small SA:V means the body surface cannot supply the metabolic needs of the interior — hence specialised exchange surfaces.
A microscopic air sac in the lung, the site of gas exchange. Its wall is a single layer of flattened type I pneumocytes in close contact with capillaries.

flashcards.front: Type I vs type II pneumocyte
flashcards.back: Type I: extremely thin, cover ~95% of the alveolar surface, adapted for gas exchange (short diffusion distance). Type II: cuboidal, secrete surfactant that lowers surface tension so alveoli do not collapse.

Inhalation is active: diaphragm contracts and flattens, external intercostals raise the ribcage, thorax volume increases, pressure falls, air flows in. Quiet exhalation is passive: muscles relax, elastic recoil reduces volume, pressure rises, air flows out.

Water flows over the gill lamellae in the OPPOSITE direction to blood flow. This keeps an oxygen concentration gradient along the WHOLE length of the lamella, so blood can keep absorbing oxygen (up to ~80–90%) — far more than a concurrent system.

Gases enter and leave through stomata (pores) whose aperture is controlled by guard cells. Inside, the spongy mesophyll has large air spaces and moist cell surfaces — a large, thin, moist internal surface for diffusion.

'Alveoli have a large surface area' is only half a mark's worth. You must LINK the adaptation to its effect: '…a large surface area, which increases the rate of diffusion.' The mark scheme rewards adaptation + effect.

Ventilation does not create oxygen. It replaces used air, maintaining a high external concentration so the concentration GRADIENT stays steep and diffusion continues. Say 'maintains the gradient', not 'adds oxygen'.

quickCheck.prompt: According to Fick's law, the rate of diffusion across an exchange surface increases when:
options: The diffusion distance increases
The surface area decreases
The concentration difference across the surface increases
The surface becomes drier
quickCheck.answer: The concentration difference across the surface increases

quickCheck.prompt: What is the role of ventilation at a gas-exchange surface?
options: It manufactures oxygen for the blood
It replaces the air so the concentration gradient is maintained
It warms the air before diffusion
It thickens the surface to slow water loss
quickCheck.answer: It replaces the air so the concentration gradient is maintained

quickCheck.prompt: A gas-exchange surface must be moist because:
options: Water carries the gases into the cells by active transport
Gases must dissolve in a liquid film before they can diffuse across the membrane
Moisture reduces the surface area needed
Water increases the diffusion distance
quickCheck.answer: Gases must dissolve in a liquid film before they can diffuse across the membrane

quickCheck.prompt: Why do larger organisms need specialised gas-exchange surfaces while a single-celled organism does not?
options: Larger organisms have a higher surface-area-to-volume ratio
Larger organisms have a lower surface-area-to-volume ratio, so the body surface cannot supply the interior by diffusion alone
Diffusion does not occur in large organisms
Small organisms cannot carry out respiration
quickCheck.answer: Larger organisms have a lower surface-area-to-volume ratio, so the body surface cannot supply the interior by diffusion alone

quickCheck.prompt: Counter-current flow makes fish gills efficient because:
options: Water and blood flow in the same direction, equalising oxygen quickly
Blood flows faster than the water
Water and blood flow in opposite directions, maintaining an oxygen gradient along the whole lamella
The gills warm the water to speed diffusion
quickCheck.answer: Water and blood flow in opposite directions, maintaining an oxygen gradient along the whole lamella

faq.q: What is the difference between ventilation, gas exchange and cell respiration?
faq.a: They are three distinct processes that are easy to confuse. Ventilation is the physical movement of air (or water) in and out — breathing — and its job is to keep the concentration gradient steep at the exchange surface. Gas exchange is the diffusion of oxygen and carbon dioxide across that surface, for example between alveoli and capillaries. Cell respiration is the chemical process inside mitochondria that uses oxygen to release energy as ATP and produces carbon dioxide as waste. Ventilation enables gas exchange, which supplies the oxygen for respiration — but naming the wrong one in an answer loses marks.

faq.q: Why is counter-current flow in fish so much better than the human lung's arrangement?
faq.a: In a counter-current system, water flows over the gill lamellae in the opposite direction to the blood. This means that all along the lamella the water always has a slightly higher oxygen concentration than the adjacent blood, so a diffusion gradient is maintained over the entire length and blood can keep absorbing oxygen right to the end — extracting up to about 80–90% of the oxygen in the water. If the two flowed in the same direction (concurrent), the concentrations would quickly equalise partway along and diffusion would stop, so far less oxygen would be absorbed.

faq.q: How do the same five properties show up in a leaf, which has no lungs or gills?
faq.a: A leaf follows exactly the same rules. The large surface area and short diffusion distance come from the spongy mesophyll, which is riddled with air spaces so gases can reach the moist surfaces of many cells. The surface is moist because the mesophyll cell walls are covered by a thin film of water. Gases are exchanged through stomata — pores whose aperture is controlled by guard cells — which lets the leaf open the pathway for carbon dioxide in daylight and close it to limit water loss. The plant does not ventilate actively, but the concentration gradient is maintained because photosynthesis and respiration continually consume and produce the gases inside the leaf.

faq.q: Why can't a large animal just exchange gases across its skin like an amoeba?
faq.a: An amoeba is tiny, so it has a very high surface-area-to-volume ratio and a short distance from its surface to every part of its cytoplasm — diffusion across the membrane is enough. As an organism gets bigger, its volume (and so its oxygen demand) rises with the cube of its size while its surface area rises only with the square, so the ratio falls. The outer surface becomes far too small, and the diffusion path to the innermost cells far too long, to supply the interior. The solution is a specialised internal surface with a huge area and a thin wall, served by ventilation and a circulatory system.

sections.content: Hold your breath and the problem announces itself within seconds: every one of your trillions of cells needs a steady supply of oxygen and a way to dump carbon dioxide, and both gases move only by diffusion. Diffusion is wonderfully fast across a fraction of a millimetre and hopelessly slow across a whole body. That single fact shapes the design of every gas-exchange surface in biology — the human alveolus, the fish gill and the leaf all obey the same handful of rules. This lesson sets out those rules, explains why body size forces organisms to obey them, and then reads them off three real surfaces. Because B3.1 is examined largely through explain questions, every worked example is laid out the way marks are actually awarded — each adaptation tied to its effect on diffusion.
What makes a gas-exchange surface effective

Gases cross any living exchange surface by diffusion alone, so a good surface is one that makes diffusion as fast as possible. The rate of diffusion depends on three things, summarised by Fick's law: it rises with the surface area available, rises with the concentration difference across the surface, and falls as the diffusion distance increases. Every property of a real exchange surface is a way of pushing one of those factors in the favourable direction. There are five properties to know, and it pays to remember not just what they are but which factor each one improves.

items: **Large surface area** — more area means more molecules can cross at once, increasing the rate of diffusion.
**Thin (short diffusion distance)** — a wall one cell thick means gases travel a shorter path, so they cross faster.
**Moist** — gases must dissolve in a thin film of liquid before they can diffuse across the cell membrane.
**Permeable** — the surface is made of thin, permeable membranes so oxygen and carbon dioxide can pass through freely.
**Maintained concentration gradient** — ventilation renews the medium outside and blood flow renews the fluid inside, keeping the concentration difference steep so diffusion does not slow and stop.

sections.content: The last property is the one students most often state poorly. Ventilation and blood flow do not add or make oxygen; they replace the gas that has already diffused. Fresh air brings a high oxygen concentration to the outside of the surface while blood carries oxygen-loaded haemoglobin away on the inside, so the difference across the surface — the gradient that drives diffusion — is continually restored. Without that renewal, the two sides would equalise and net diffusion would cease.
\text{rate of diffusion} \;\propto\; \frac{\text{surface area} \times \text{concentration difference}}{\text{diffusion distance}}
Surface-area-to-volume ratio and why size matters

A single-celled organism such as an amoeba exchanges gases straight across its cell membrane — no lungs, no gills, no blood. It can do this because it is tiny. The reason is surface-area-to-volume ratio. As an object grows, its volume increases with the cube of its length while its surface area increases only with the square, so the ratio of surface to volume falls steadily. A large organism therefore has a relatively small outer surface for its bulk, and the distance from that surface to its innermost cells becomes far too great for diffusion to supply them. Metabolically active animals like mammals make the mismatch worse still, because their high respiration rate demands a large oxygen supply. The evolutionary answer is a specialised gas-exchange surface — folded or branched to pack a huge area into a small space, kept thin and moist — served by a ventilation system that renews the medium and a circulatory system that distributes the gases to every cell.

items: Volume (and so metabolic demand) rises faster than surface area as an organism grows, so SA:V falls.
A low SA:V means the body surface cannot supply the interior, and diffusion distances become too long.
Large or active organisms therefore need a specialised exchange surface with a large area and short diffusion path.
Ventilation maintains the gradient at that surface; a circulatory system carries gases the rest of the way.

sections.question: A cube-shaped organism has sides of length 1 mm. A larger cube-shaped organism has sides of length 4 mm. (a) Calculate the surface-area-to-volume ratio of each. (b) Explain what your answer shows about the need for a specialised gas-exchange surface. [4]
sections.solution:
**(a) Surface-area-to-volume ratio of each cube.**
For a cube of side $L$: surface area $= 6L^2$, volume $= L^3$, so SA:V $= 6L^2 / L^3 = 6/L$.

Small cube ($L = 1$ mm): SA:V $= 6/1 = 6\,\text{mm}^{-1}$ (surface area $6\,\text{mm}^2$, volume $1\,\text{mm}^3$, ratio $6:1$). [M1]

Large cube ($L = 4$ mm): SA:V $= 6/4 = 1.5\,\text{mm}^{-1}$ (surface area $96\,\text{mm}^2$, volume $64\,\text{mm}^3$, ratio $1.5:1$). [A1]

**(b) What this shows.**
The larger organism has a much SMALLER surface area relative to its volume (1.5:1 versus 6:1). [M1] Its outer surface is therefore too small, and the diffusion path to its central cells too long, to supply enough oxygen and remove carbon dioxide by diffusion across the body surface alone — so a larger organism needs a specialised gas-exchange surface (with a large area and short diffusion distance) and a transport system. [A1]

*Notice the ratio fell by exactly the factor by which the side length grew (×4), because SA:V $= 6/L$. This is the whole reason body size forces specialised surfaces to evolve.*

sections.content: Gas exchange in the human lungs: the alveolus

In humans the gas-exchange surface is the collection of alveoli — hundreds of millions of microscopic air sacs clustered like grapes at the ends of the bronchioles. Together they turn the five general properties into concrete anatomy. Their sheer number gives a combined surface area of roughly 70–100 m², about the size of a tennis court, packed inside the chest. The wall of each alveolus is a single layer of extremely thin, flattened type I pneumocytes, pressed against capillaries whose walls are also one cell thick, so the total diffusion distance for a gas molecule is only about half a micrometre. A dense network of capillaries wraps every alveolus, so blood constantly carries oxygen away and brings carbon dioxide in, maintaining a steep concentration gradient. The inner surface is coated with a thin film of moisture in which gases dissolve, and type II pneumocytes secrete surfactant into that film to lower surface tension so the alveoli do not collapse. Read together, these are exactly the properties of an effective exchange surface — large area, short diffusion distance, rich blood supply and a moist, permeable membrane.

items: **Large total surface area** — hundreds of millions of alveoli give ~70–100 m² for diffusion.
**Thin walls** — single layer of type I pneumocytes plus a one-cell-thick capillary wall, so diffusion distance is only ~0.5 µm.
**Dense capillary network** — blood flow renews the gases inside, maintaining a steep concentration gradient.
**Moist surface with surfactant** — gases dissolve before diffusing; surfactant lowers surface tension so alveoli stay open.

sections.content: The mechanism of ventilation

The alveoli themselves cannot move air; ventilation is driven by changing the volume of the thoracic cavity, which changes the pressure inside the lungs. During inhalation the diaphragm contracts and flattens and the external intercostal muscles contract to pull the ribcage up and out. Both actions increase the volume of the thorax, which lowers the pressure inside the lungs below atmospheric pressure, so air flows in — an active process requiring muscle contraction. Quiet exhalation is largely passive: the diaphragm and external intercostals relax, and the elastic recoil of the lung tissue reduces the thoracic volume, raising the internal pressure above atmospheric so air flows out. During vigorous exercise, exhalation becomes active too, with the internal intercostal muscles and the abdominal muscles contracting to push air out faster. Throughout, the point of breathing is to keep renewing the air at the alveolar surface so the oxygen and carbon dioxide gradients stay steep.

A very common exam task is to compare inhalation and exhalation. Structure the answer around four rows — diaphragm action, intercostal action, thorax volume, and lung pressure — and state clearly which process is active and which is passive. Antagonistic muscle pairs (external vs internal intercostals) are worth naming explicitly.

sections.question: A person at rest has a tidal volume of 0.50 dm³ and breathes 12 times per minute. During exercise their tidal volume rises to 3.0 dm³ and their breathing rate to 30 breaths per minute. (a) Calculate the ventilation rate at rest and during exercise. (b) Explain, in terms of the concentration gradient, why the change helps gas exchange. [4]
sections.solution:
**(a) Ventilation rate = tidal volume × breathing rate.** [M1: correct formula]

At rest: $0.50\,\text{dm}^3 \times 12\,\text{min}^{-1} = 6.0\,\text{dm}^3\,\text{min}^{-1}$.

During exercise: $3.0\,\text{dm}^3 \times 30\,\text{min}^{-1} = 90\,\text{dm}^3\,\text{min}^{-1}$. [A1: both values with units]

**(b) Why this helps gas exchange.**
The faster, deeper breathing replaces the air in the alveoli more rapidly, keeping the oxygen concentration in the alveoli high and the carbon dioxide concentration low. [M1] This maintains a steeper concentration gradient between the alveoli and the blood, so oxygen and carbon dioxide diffuse faster — meeting the higher demand of respiring muscles during exercise. [A1]

*Ventilation rate rose 15-fold (from 6.0 to 90 dm³ min⁻¹). Note the explanation credits maintaining the GRADIENT, not 'adding more oxygen' — that phrasing is what the mark scheme wants.*

sections.content: Comparison: fish gills and counter-current flow

Fish face the same problem in water, which holds far less oxygen than air, and they solve it with gills. Each gill is made of many filaments carrying thin, folded lamellae that provide a large, thin, moist surface richly supplied with blood — the familiar properties again. Their special trick is counter-current flow: water is pumped over the lamellae in the opposite direction to the blood flowing through them. Because the two flow the opposite way, the water always has a slightly higher oxygen concentration than the adjacent blood along the entire length of the lamella, so a favourable gradient is maintained the whole way and blood keeps absorbing oxygen right to the end — extracting up to around 80–90% of the oxygen in the water. If water and blood flowed in the same direction (concurrent), their concentrations would equalise partway along and diffusion would stop, so much less oxygen would be taken up. Counter-current exchange is simply a highly efficient way of keeping the concentration gradient steep.
Comparison: gas exchange in a leaf

Plants have no muscles to ventilate and no blood to circulate, yet a leaf is an effective gas-exchange surface built on the same principles. Gases enter and leave through stomata — pores mostly on the underside of the leaf — whose aperture is opened and closed by a pair of guard cells, allowing the plant to admit carbon dioxide in daylight for photosynthesis while limiting water loss. Inside the leaf, the spongy mesophyll is a loose tissue riddled with large air spaces, so diffusing gases can reach the surfaces of many cells: this provides the large surface area and short diffusion distances. Those cell surfaces are covered by a thin film of water, satisfying the moist requirement, and the concentration gradient is maintained not by ventilation but by the cells themselves — photosynthesis continually consuming carbon dioxide and releasing oxygen in the light, and respiration doing the reverse. The medium is not actively pumped, but the properties of an effective surface are all present.

items: **Human lung:** alveoli give a huge internal area; air is actively ventilated; blood flow maintains the gradient.
**Fish gill:** folded lamellae give a large area; counter-current flow of water and blood keeps the gradient steep the whole length.
**Leaf:** stomata (controlled by guard cells) admit gases; spongy mesophyll gives a large, moist internal surface; photosynthesis/respiration maintain the gradient.
**Shared logic:** every case maximises area and gradient and minimises diffusion distance — the same rules, different anatomy.

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Describing an adaptation without stating its effect** — 'large surface area' alone is incomplete; you must add 'which increases the rate of diffusion'. The mark scheme rewards adaptation + effect.
**Saying ventilation 'adds' or 'increases' oxygen** — ventilation replaces used air to MAINTAIN the concentration gradient; it does not create oxygen. Use the word 'gradient'.
**Confusing ventilation, gas exchange and respiration** — breathing is a physical process, gas exchange is diffusion across a surface, respiration is a chemical process in cells. Name the right one.
**Forgetting why the surface must be moist** — gases have to DISSOLVE before they can diffuse across the membrane; do not say the water 'carries' the gas by active transport.

**Getting counter-current flow backwards** — water and blood flow in OPPOSITE directions; the benefit is maintaining an oxygen gradient along the whole lamella, not making blood and water flow together.
**Getting the SA:V logic inverted** — larger organisms have a LOWER surface-area-to-volume ratio, which is why they need specialised surfaces. Students who write 'higher' lose the mark.
**Confusing type I and type II pneumocytes** — type I are the thin cells where exchange happens; type II secrete surfactant. Only surfactant lowers surface tension.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

Explain questions in B3.1 are marked analytically: each distinct, valid point earns one mark, up to the maximum available. For an adaptation question the engine is looking for a named structural adaptation LINKED to its effect on diffusion — the link is what turns a description into an explanation. Method marks (M) credit a correct adaptation, answer marks (A) credit the effect it produces, error-carried-forward (ECF) means the points stand independently, and equivalent correct wording is accepted. Study how each mark below is tied to a specific adaptation-and-effect pair rather than to loose phrasing.

sections.question: Explain how the structure of the alveoli adapts them for efficient gas exchange. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
The millions of alveoli together provide a very large total surface area, which increases the rate of diffusion of gases. The wall of each alveolus is a single layer of flattened (type I) cells, and the surrounding capillary wall is also one cell thick, giving a very short diffusion distance so gases cross quickly. Each alveolus is surrounded by a dense network of capillaries, so blood continually removes oxygen and delivers carbon dioxide, maintaining a steep concentration gradient across the surface. The inner surface is moist, so oxygen and carbon dioxide dissolve before diffusing across the permeable membranes into and out of the blood.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks** — each distinct valid adaptation LINKED to its effect scores 1 (maximum 4):

- **Point 1:** large total surface area (M) → increases rate of diffusion (A).
- **Point 2:** thin walls / single layer of cells / short diffusion distance (M) → gases diffuse across faster (A).
- **Point 3:** dense capillary network / good blood supply (M) → maintains a steep concentration gradient (A).
- **Point 4:** moist / permeable surface (M) → gases dissolve and diffuse across the membrane (A).

**The link is compulsory.** A bare list — 'large surface area, thin walls, moist, good blood supply' — is a description, not an explanation, and the engine credits it at most partially. Each mark needs the adaptation AND its effect on diffusion; that is exactly how a human examiner applies this mark scheme.

**Equivalents accepted.** 'One-cell-thick wall', 'flattened epithelium', 'type I pneumocytes' all count as the thin-wall point; 'rich blood supply', 'many capillaries' all count as the gradient point. The engine matches the idea, not the exact words.

**ECF and partial credit.** The four points are independent, so missing one does not cost the others — a student who omits the moist-surface point but links the other three correctly still scores 3. There is no all-or-nothing penalty; each valid adaptation-plus-effect line stands on its own.

**Bottom line:** to secure all 4 marks, give four DIFFERENT adaptations, and for each one say what it does for diffusion. Repeating the same idea in two forms scores once.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — point by point, with ECF and a cap at the marks available. So write in distinct adaptation-plus-effect statements: name the feature, then say what it does for diffusion (larger area → faster diffusion; thinner wall → shorter path; more capillaries → steeper gradient; moist surface → gases dissolve). Listing features without their effects is the single most common way to throw away marks on this topic.
Where this leads

The logic of B3.1 — large area, short distance, moist and permeable surface, maintained gradient — is a template you will reuse across the whole of form and function. It reappears in the structure of the small intestine (villi and microvilli for absorption), in the nephron and the capillary beds of the circulatory system, and in the root hairs and leaf of a plant. Master the habit of naming an adaptation and then stating its effect on the rate of transport, and you have a way of answering exchange-surface questions for any organ in any organism.
